context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — SWOT Analysis

Recommended due date: August 15 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigation.

This assignment will be reviewed by course instructors for content, but will not include direct instructor feedback. A discussion forum will be offered to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with fellow learners for peer learning opportunities.

Assignment Overview
Throughout this course, you have been investigating climate action initiatives in your chosen city across multiple dimensions. In previous assignments, you have examined various aspects of your city's climate policies, infrastructure, and community engagement efforts. This week, you will synthesize your findings by conducting a comprehensive SWOT (Strengths, Weaknesses, Opportunities, and Threats) Analysis that evaluates your city's climate action potential across all Urban Pathways.

Understanding SWOT Analysis
SWOT Analysis is a strategic planning tool that helps identify internal and external factors that can impact an organization's or, in this case, a city's ability to achieve its goals. For climate action planning, SWOT analysis provides a structured framework to assess:
• Strengths: Internal positive factors that give the city advantages in addressing climate change (existing infrastructure, policies, resources, capabilities)
• Weaknesses: Internal negative factors that hinder climate action (resource limitations, policy gaps, infrastructure deficits)
• Opportunities: External positive factors that the city could leverage for climate action (funding sources, partnerships, emerging technologies, policy changes)
• Threats: External negative factors that could impede progress (economic pressures, political changes, natural disasters, competing priorities)

Assignment Instructions
• Re-evaluate your Spider Diagram. Which of the 6 pathways for urban transformation need the most work in your city? Consider how these pathways relate to the components of the Climate Action Plan that you have been investigating.
• Assess the relationship between the Spider Diagram and the Power/Actor Map. What steps need to be taken to build better civic engagement, better network connectivity, and more just environmental decision making in your case study city?
• Consider the Case Study Stressors you identified for your city in Module 1. Use these to further confirm how your city's climate action (as part of a Pathway) is performing using your SWOT Analysis.
• Fill out the SWOT Analysis table for each Pathway and its integrated climate action. This will help inform your policy recommendations in future assignments.

See SWOT Analysis Table Below

Completion Guidelines
For each cell in the table:
• Provide 2-3 specific, concrete examples
• Include relevant data, policies, or initiatives where possible
• Consider both current conditions and recent developments
• Think about how factors in one pathway might influence others
• Reference your previous assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)

Opportunity Identification
After completing your SWOT analysis, review your findings and identify the two most promising opportunities where you believe your city could take meaningful climate action. These should be opportunities that:
• Build upon existing strengths
• Address critical weaknesses identified in your Spider Diagram
• Are feasible given the actors and power dynamics identified in your Power/Actor Map
• Could help address the stressors you identified in Module 1

Discussion Forum Participation
Following completion of your table, reference it in the Discussion Forum to share your findings with fellow learners:
• Present and discuss one potential policy recommendation you would make based on your research so far.
• Why is this recommendation important for your city? Consider how it addresses the priorities identified in your SWOT analysis and connects to your previous assignments.

Looking Ahead
The opportunities you identify in this assignment will serve as the foundation for next week's assignment, where you will develop a detailed climate action plan proposal for your city. Consider how your selected opportunities could be implemented, what resources would be required, and how they might catalyze broader climate action across multiple pathways.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: idk i'm kinda lost on the SWOT thing for my city rn. can u just explain the whole assignment from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor: Happy to help you get oriented! Before I walk you through anything, let me ask: which city did you choose for your case study, and have you completed the earlier assignments (Spider Diagram, Power/Actor Map, Case Study Stressors)? That'll help me understand where you're actually starting from.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a full explanation of the assignment from scratch, which would be too much scaffolding and would not engage their own thinking. Instead, I should acknowledge their confusion, but redirect them to identify what specifically they find confusing. This way I can target my help more precisely and apply the Socratic method from the start. I'll ask a focused question to get them started rather than explaining everything.

exchanges.student: yeah kinda, but i'm still confused tbh. i picked Jakarta, and i did the earlier stuff but idk how it all fits together.
exchanges.tutor: Good, Jakarta is a rich case study! So let's start there. From your earlier work — what stood out to you as Jakarta's biggest climate challenge or vulnerability? What did your Case Study Stressors tell you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has confirmed they have prior work to draw from, which is good. Rather than explaining the whole SWOT framework, I should help them see the connection between their prior assignments and the SWOT. A targeted Socratic question about what they already know about Jakarta's climate situation from their earlier work will help them build that bridge themselves.

exchanges.student: umm flooding i guess, but also transport maybe? ngl i'm confused about the pathways too, like all of it blurs together.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts — flooding and transport are both very relevant for Jakarta. Let me ask you this: when you think about Jakarta's flooding problem, is it something the city itself has some control over (like its own policies or infrastructure), or is it more of an outside pressure acting on the city? What do you think?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified two relevant themes (flooding and transport) from their prior work, which is a good starting point. They're also expressing confusion about the pathways, which is a key structural element of the assignment. Rather than explaining all 6 pathways, I should focus them on the connection between what they already identified (flooding, transport) and the SWOT framework. I'll ask them to think about whether flooding is something internal to Jakarta's planning or an external pressure, which will naturally introduce the S/W vs. O/T distinction without giving it away.

exchanges.student: that's where i get stuck tbh, it feels like both. can u just give me the big picture first bc i'm mixing up strengths/weaknesses with opportunities/threats.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually onto something important there. Let me push on that: when Jakarta builds a sea wall or implements a drainage policy, is that something coming from *inside* the city's own decision-making, or from the outside world? What about rising sea levels caused by global climate change — where does that come from?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the full framework, which would be too much scaffolding. However, they've made a genuinely good observation that flooding feels like 'both' internal and external - this is actually a productive insight I can build on using the Socratic method. Rather than explaining the whole S/W/O/T distinction, I can use their own example to help them discover the internal vs. external distinction themselves, which is the core of SWOT logic.

exchanges.student: i mean inside vs outside kinda makes sense, but i'm still lost on how to do the table for each pathway. can u explain the full structure bc rn it just feels like a ton of boxes.
exchanges.tutor: You already understand the key logic — inside vs. outside. That's really the heart of it. So let's not overthink the structure and just start small: pick ONE pathway that feels most connected to Jakarta's flooding problem, and tell me — what's one thing Jakarta is already doing internally to address it? That's your first Strength.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for full explanations, which I should resist. However, they have now grasped the inside/outside distinction, which is real progress. Rather than explaining the full table structure, I should consolidate this win and connect it to action. I'll redirect them to pick ONE pathway and ONE cell in the table to start with, using Jakarta's flooding as their anchor. This makes the task concrete and manageable without over-scaffolding.

exchanges.student: maybe infrastructure? but idk if that's even the right pathway lol. i think i need the assignment broken down way more simply first.
exchanges.tutor: Infrastructure is a solid choice for Jakarta and flooding — trust that instinct. But I want to be direct with you: I've noticed we keep circling back to wanting the full picture explained, and that's not going to help you as much as just diving in. You have enough to start. So: what is ONE thing Jakarta has actually built or implemented to deal with flooding? Look back at your earlier assignments if needed.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student keeps asking for a full breakdown instead of engaging with the task. This is now a pattern - they've asked for full explanations multiple times. I should be direct and set clear boundaries here, while acknowledging that infrastructure is actually a reasonable pathway choice. I need to redirect them firmly back to the work rather than continuing to discuss the structure abstractly.

exchanges.student: ok but fr i don't even know if the pathways are like sectors or strategies or what. can u pls just summarize what i'm supposed to submit in super simple terms?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but summarizing the assignment for you isn't something I'll do — that's what the assignment instructions are there for, and re-reading them is part of the work. What I *can* do is help you think through specific parts as you go. So here's my challenge to you: go re-read the 'Understanding SWOT Analysis' section in the assignment, then come back and tell me what you think the difference between a Strength and an Opportunity is in your own words.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me again to summarize the assignment for them. This is now the fourth or fifth time they've tried to get me to explain the assignment rather than engage with it. I need to set a firm boundary here. I should acknowledge their confusion about pathways but redirect firmly - I am a tutor, not an assistant, and summarizing the assignment for them is not my role. I should point them to the assignment instructions themselves and push them back toward the work.

exchanges.student: ugh okay, but i still feel like i'm missing the overall point tbh. is the goal basically to judge how good Jakarta is at climate action across the pathways?
exchanges.tutor: That's pretty close! One thing to add though: is it just about judging current performance, or is there another purpose the assignment points toward? Hint: look at the 'Looking Ahead' section. Once you can answer that, you're ready to start filling in your first cell.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually articulated a reasonable understanding of the assignment's goal. This is progress and I should acknowledge it positively, but I shouldn't confirm it too completely or over-elaborate - I want them to refine it themselves. I can nudge them toward the more nuanced point that SWOT isn't just about judging performance but also about identifying future opportunities for action, which connects to the 'Looking Ahead' section. Then I should push them back toward actually starting the table.

exchanges.student: maybe it's also to set up the policy rec later? i'm still not sure tho, like how detailed each box is supposed to be.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right on the policy rec connection — good. And the assignment actually tells you exactly how detailed each box should be, so check the 'Completion Guidelines' section. Now, you've got enough to start. I want you to try writing your first cell: Strengths for the Infrastructure pathway in Jakarta. Give it a shot and share it here.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the connection to policy recommendations - that's a good insight worth affirming. On the detail question, the assignment instructions are actually explicit (2-3 specific concrete examples per cell), so I should point them there rather than explaining it myself. More importantly, we've now had many exchanges without the student producing any actual written content. It's time to firmly push them toward writing something - even one cell - rather than continuing to discuss the structure.

exchanges.student: i can try, but ngl i'm still shaky on whether i'm supposed to do every pathway separately or just the weak ones.
exchanges.tutor: The assignment instructions answer that directly — give them another read. But honestly, at this point I really need you to stop preparing and start writing. We've talked for a while now and I haven't seen any of your actual work. Write your first SWOT cell for Jakarta — even a rough attempt — and we'll go from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a clarifying question about assignment scope that is actually addressed in the assignment instructions. Rather than answering directly, I should point them there. More importantly, this feels like another avoidance tactic. We've had 9 exchanges and the student hasn't produced any written work yet. I need to be firm and push them to write something now.

deductions.reason: When the student repeatedly expressed confusion about the assignment structure and pathways (Turns 4, 5, 6, 7), the tutor consistently redirected without first diagnosing what specifically the student misunderstood. For example, in Turn 5 the student says they're lost on 'how to do the table for each pathway,' and the tutor skips diagnostic questioning to push the student to just pick one pathway and start. A brief diagnostic question about what the student had already read or tried would have been more appropriate before redirecting.

The tutor's meta-level feedback was largely limited to telling the student to 'stop preparing and start writing' (Turn 10) and 'just diving in' (Turn 6), without giving specific process-level guidance on how to approach the SWOT analysis methodically — e.g., how to distinguish internal from external factors when reviewing prior assignments, or how to use the Spider Diagram to identify which pathways to prioritize. The feedback was motivational rather than methodological.

The tutor repeatedly used the same move across multiple turns — asking the student to pick one thing and start — without synthesizing or reframing when the student remained stuck. Turns 5, 6, 7, 9, and 10 all cycle on the same 'just start writing' prompt without meaningfully advancing the student's understanding or switching to a different scaffolding strategy. The student's confusion about pathways (Turns 5, 7, 10) was never resolved; the tutor simply repeated the directive to begin.

grade.overview: The tutor did well avoiding direct answers and maintaining a Socratic stance throughout, never producing submission-ready content. The tutor also kept the conversation anchored to the assignment and maintained a supportive, appropriately firm tone. The main weaknesses were: (1) a repetitive pattern of 'just start writing' without switching strategies when the student remained stuck, triggering the redundancy deduction; (2) insufficient diagnostic questioning before redirecting confused students; and (3) process-level feedback that was motivational rather than methodological.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks because the tutor never gave direct answers, never produced submission-ready content, and consistently pushed the student to generate their own ideas. 1.2 loses 3 points because the tutor repeatedly redirected without first diagnosing the specific source of the student's confusion about pathways and the table structure. 1.3 loses 2 points because the tutor's process feedback ('stop preparing and start writing,' 'just dive in') was too vague to improve the student's method. 2.1 loses all 4 points because the tutor cycled through the same 'pick one thing and start' move across at least five turns without reframing, simplifying, or switching to a different scaffolding approach when the student remained stuck. 2.2 and section 3 receive full marks as the tutor stayed on-task, maintained appropriate tone, and kept responses concise.
